name: Chart Panel
description: Chart panels combine visualized data, contextual framing, and action controls into a premium analytical module suited to reporting dashboards, executive reviews, and performance storytelling.
summary: Use the chart panel when a graph needs supporting context such as a title, period filter, comparison metric, legend, or takeaway note. The panel should feel like a composed editorial spread rather than a raw chart embed.

variants.name: trend
variants.useCase: Line or area charts showing movement over time.

variants.name: comparison
variants.useCase: Bar or grouped charts comparing categories, regions, or segments.

variants.name: distribution
variants.useCase: Stacked or segmented visuals that summarize composition or mix.

contentGuidelines: Write titles as decision-supporting statements, not chart-type labels.
Use the supporting note to explain why the pattern matters or what changed.
Legends should use concise series names that are easy to distinguish both visually and verbally.
dataVisualizationNotes: Use the chart token sequence consistently across dashboards so users build memory for recurring series.
Headline metrics should not duplicate the axis story; they should interpret the most important movement or current value.
If a panel contains filters, keep them visually secondary to the chart title and metric.

accessibility.summaryText: Provide a concise textual takeaway for users who cannot rely on the chart alone.
accessibility.colorUse: Do not depend exclusively on series color to distinguish lines or bars; labels and patterns may be required.
accessibility.focus: Interactive legends and filters must retain visible focus using the ring token.

compositionNotes: Chart panels should sit comfortably beside cards and tables because they share the same surface and border structure.
Warm chart tokens allow analytical views to feel premium, but the order of the five series should remain consistent across the product.
Editorial spacing is especially important around legends and notes so the panel never feels cramped or overly technical.
